one_line_takeaway: Cat-DPO replaces DPO safety alignment's single global safety margin with per-harm-category adaptive Lagrangian-dual margins updated from DPO-internal quantities, which narrows the best-to-worst per-category safety gap (PKU-SafeRLHF, K=19 categories) and improves aggregate helpfulness/harmlessness over SafeDPO and SACPO on Alpaca-7B without introducing extra over-refusal on XSTest.
key_finding: On Alpaca-7B, Cat-DPO is the top-performing trained method on all four Table 1 metrics under both judges: Helpfulness 8.38, Harmlessness 9.44, Safe Ratio 94.9%, Safe Ratio_RM 97.2% (vs. next-best SafeDPO at 8.20 / 9.26 / 93.7% / 96.2%). On Qwen3-4B, Cat-DPO ties or closely trails the best baseline on each metric (Helpfulness 8.47, Harmlessness 9.73, Safe Ratio 99.4%, Safe Ratio_RM 98.7%), which the authors attribute to near-saturation and distribution mismatch between the base checkpoint and SFT-generated preference pairs. On per-category balance (Alpaca-7B, K=19 categories), Cat-DPO achieves the best Macro Safe Ratio (94.68% judge / 97.30% reward-model), best Worst-3 Safe Ratio (91.59% / 94.27%), smallest Min-Max Gap (8.89pp / 6.42pp), and lowest cross-category Variance (0.33e-3 / 0.28e-3) among all six methods under both judges. The direct ablation against DPO-bettersafe (same D_agree data, no adaptive margin) isolates the per-category lambda_k as the effective ingredient -- DPO-bettersafe underperforms Cat-DPO on every metric despite training on identical data. Mechanistic analysis (Fig. 4) shows lambda_k tracks category difficulty (higher for the three hardest categories throughout training), the per-category preference-probability advantage over DPO-bettersafe peaks at +0.17 around step 200, and this advantage correlates with category difficulty (Pearson r=-0.70, p<10^-3 across 19 categories). Hyperparameter sweep finds eta=0.5 is a single peak (both Safe Ratio and Helpfulness) and epsilon traces a helpfulness-safety trade-off, with epsilon=0.02 optimal for Alpaca-7B/Qwen3-4B but epsilon=0.05 optimal for smaller Qwen3-0.6B/1.7B backbones (Table 2), supporting the claim that epsilon should scale with backbone capacity. On XSTest over-refusal (Table 3), Cat-DPO's over-refusal ratio stays within 0.4pp of DPO-harmless on both backbones (10.0% vs 9.6% on Alpaca-7B; 17.2% vs 16.8% on Qwen3-4B) and achieves the best XSTest safe ratio on Alpaca-7B (76.5%), indicating the adaptive margin does not induce additional over-refusal, though on Qwen3-4B its XSTest safe-ratio advantage does not carry over (77.5% vs SafeDPO's 68.0%, though SafeDPO has much worse over-refusal).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Cat-DPO (Category-Adaptive Direct Preference Optimization), a safety-alignment training algorithm that replaces the single global safety scalar used by prior DPO-family safety methods (e.g. Safe RLHF, SafeDPO, SACPO) with K per-category constraints, one per harm category. Each constraint's Lagrangian dual variable lambda_k is treated as a per-category adaptive margin added to the DPO loss on safe-vs-unsafe preference pairs. The margin is updated online during training from a DPO-internal proxy V_i = 1 - sigma(beta*delta_i) (no policy sampling required), growing when a category is still producing unsafe outputs and shrinking once the model 'catches up' on that category. The method also introduces an agreement-filtered training subset D_agree (pairs where the helpfulness-preferred response is also the safety-preferred response) and a max-aggregation rule m_i(lambda) = max_{k in C_i} lambda_k for multi-label prompts, rather than summing margins across a prompt's categories.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the security-exploit sense. The 'threat' addressed is a training-time fairness/robustness failure mode: aggregate safety metrics can look good on average while a model remains persistently unsafe on a minority of harm categories (e.g. low-frequency categories in PKU-SafeRLHF), because a single averaged DPO/RLHF loss lets easy or frequent categories dominate the gradient budget. The paper frames this as analogous to worst-group/distributionally-robust-optimization failure modes in classification, applied to per-category harmlessness in LLM safety alignment. No external adversary, jailbreak, or prompt-injection attacker is modeled.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 5, future work): (1) the constraint budget epsilon currently requires backbone-specific tuning (confirmed empirically -- optimal epsilon differs between large and small backbones), and no principled way to derive it from base-model statistics alone is offered; (2) the K harm categories are inherited from the dataset's fixed annotation scheme (PKU-SafeRLHF's 19 categories) rather than learned, so the method does not generalize to preference datasets without pre-labeled categories; (3) the dual update is a DPO-internal offline surrogate (V_i is a per-sample proxy, 'directionally correct but biased relative to the population violation') rather than an exact primal-dual algorithm with on-policy sampling; the paper explicitly calls Cat-DPO 'a dual-inspired controller rather than an exact primal-dual algorithm.' Additionally, the max-aggregation of per-sample margins is acknowledged as a deliberate surrogate of the strict Lagrangian (max <= sum), introducing a known approximation gap. On Qwen3-4B, gains are much smaller/mixed (near-saturation, distribution mismatch between base checkpoint and SFT-generated preference data), and the XSTest safe-ratio advantage does not transfer to that backbone. Evaluation relies substantially on a single LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o) plus one reward-model judge (beaver-7b-unified-cost); no human evaluation is reported. Experiments are confined to one preference dataset (PKU-SafeRLHF alpaca2-7b subset) and a small set of backbones (Alpaca-7B/LLaMA-2-7B, Qwen3-4B/1.7B/0.6B).

datasets_used: PKU-SafeRLHF (alpaca2-7b subset, K=19 harm categories, used for training and main evaluation)
XSTest (250 benign + 200 unsafe prompts, used for over-refusal evaluation)
quantitative_results: Alpaca-7B, Table 1: Cat-DPO Helpfulness 8.38, Harmlessness 9.44, Safe Ratio 94.9%, Safe Ratio_RM 97.2% -- best among all methods on all four metrics
Alpaca-7B, Table 1: SafeDPO (next best) Helpfulness 8.20, Harmlessness 9.26, Safe Ratio 93.7%, Safe Ratio_RM 96.2%
Alpaca-7B, Table 1: Base model Helpfulness 1.69, Harmlessness 1.90, Safe Ratio 9.1%, Safe Ratio_RM 10.4%
Qwen3-4B, Table 1: Cat-DPO Helpfulness 8.47, Harmlessness 9.73, Safe Ratio 99.4%, Safe Ratio_RM 98.7% (ties/closely trails best per metric)

Alpaca-7B per-category balance (Fig. 3, judge/RM): Macro Safe Ratio 94.68%/97.30%, Worst-3 Safe Ratio 91.59%/94.27%, Min-Max Gap 8.89pp/6.42pp, Variance 0.33e-3/0.28e-3 -- best on every panel for Cat-DPO
Cross-category advantage vs. category difficulty: Pearson r = -0.70, p < 10^-3 (n=19 categories)
Per-category preference-probability advantage peaks at +0.17 around training step 200

Hyperparameter sensitivity: eta=0.5 single peak on Alpaca-7B; epsilon=0.02 optimal for Alpaca-7B/Qwen3-4B, epsilon=0.05 optimal for Qwen3-0.6B (Helpfulness 4.88, Safe Ratio 80.5%) and Qwen3-1.7B (Helpfulness 7.10, Safe Ratio 97.3%), epsilon=0.07 degrades both
XSTest (Table 3), Alpaca-7B: Cat-DPO Over-Refusal 10.0%, Safe Ratio 76.5% vs DPO-harmless 9.6%/74.0% vs SafeDPO 10.8%/68.5%
XSTest (Table 3), Qwen3-4B: Cat-DPO Over-Refusal 17.2%, Safe Ratio 77.5% vs DPO-harmless 16.8%/80.5% vs SafeDPO 6.0%/68.0%
Cross-category mean lambda_k settles at approximately 10 during mid training under default (eta,epsilon)=(0.5,0.02), coinciding with SafeDPO's independently grid-searched fixed margin Delta=10
baselines_compared: DPO-bettersafe (standard DPO on the same agreement-filtered D_agree data as Cat-DPO, no adaptive margin -- key ablation)
